description.summary: A woman cursed with a second mouth on the back of her head that demands constant feeding. The second mouth can speak, scream, and manipulate the woman's hair like tentacles to feed itself, creating a horrifying domestic nightmare.
description.full: Futakuchi-onna (二口女, 'two-mouthed woman') is a Japanese yokai depicting a woman with a normal appearance from the front but possessing a second mouth on the back of her skull, hidden beneath her hair. This second mouth has teeth, lips, and the ability to speak—often screaming demands for food. The hair around the mouth can move independently like tentacles, enabling the mouth to feed itself. The condition typically arises as a curse, most commonly from the story of a stingy stepmother who starved her stepchild to death; the child's vengeful spirit creates the second mouth that consumes twice as much food, punishing the stepmother's miserliness. Alternatively, some versions present futakuchi-onna as born yokai or women cursed for their own stinginess. The yokai embodies themes of hidden hunger, punishment for cruelty, and domestic horror.

etymology.origin: From Japanese 'futa' (二, two), 'kuchi' (口, mouth), and 'onna' (女, woman)

physical.front_appearance: Normal human woman, often described as attractive or ordinary
physical.back_appearance: Second mouth located on back of skull under hair

features.description: Normal human appearance, no visible abnormality from front
Fully formed mouth on back of head with teeth, lips, tongue
Hair around second mouth moves like tentacles or snakes
Actual opening in skull where mouth is located

physical.morphology: Otherwise normal human woman with supernatural additional mouth

abilities.description: Hair around second mouth acts as prehensile tentacles to grasp food
The back mouth can speak independently, usually to demand food or scream
Must consume food for both mouths—roughly double normal intake
Second mouth can threaten, curse, or emotionally torment the woman

second_mouth_behavior: Threatens the woman if not fed
Uses hair to snatch food independently
Causes intense pain (described as headaches or skull-splitting agony)
woman_behavior: Attempts to hide the second mouth
Must acquire twice the normal food

curse_variations.description: Most common version: Stingy stepmother remarries to save money on food, starves stepchild to death. Child's vengeful spirit creates second mouth that eats twice as much, punishing the stepmother.
Woman who never fed anyone or was excessively stingy with food is cursed with ravenous second mouth
Some versions present futakuchi-onna as born yokai, not cursed humans
Woman who ate secretly or gluttonously is cursed with visible second mouth

curse_mechanics.manifestation: Second mouth appears on back of skull
curse_mechanics.removal: Rarely described; sometimes requires atonement or exorcism

range.description: Japanese folklore tradition, particularly Edo period tales
regions: Throughout Japan, particularly in regional folk tales

origins.cultural_roots: Edo period kaidan (怪談, ghost stories), cautionary tales about domestic virtues
origins.earliest_accounts: Edo period folktales and oral traditions
origins.folklore_connections: Part of broader cautionary tale tradition in Japanese folklore

timeline.significance: Becomes established yokai in folk tradition

timeline.event: Featured in yokai compilations and popular media
timeline.significance: Remains popular in horror manga and anime

moral_lessons: Feed the hungry, especially children in your care
Cruelty to stepchildren brings supernatural punishment

representations: Punishment fitting the crime (starvation → endless hunger)

indigenous.cultural_sensitivity: ⚠️ IMPORTANT: Futakuchi-onna stories reflect historical attitudes toward stepfamilies, women, and domestic roles in feudal Japan. Approach with cultural-historical context.

popular_culture.influence: Popular in Japanese horror (J-horror) for its visceral body horror elements

regional.variation: Specific details of how second mouth feeds, speaks, or torments vary by telling

modern_interpretations.description: Modern retellings emphasize psychological torment and body horror aspects
Contemporary analysis sees themes of female hunger, suppression, and domestic oppression

similar_yokai.name: Kuchisake-onna
similar_yokai.similarity: Both involve mouths as source of horror
similar_yokai.difference: Kuchisake-onna has slit mouth, is urban legend; futakuchi-onna has second mouth, is traditional folklore

similar_yokai.name: Rokurokubi
similar_yokai.similarity: Both appear as normal women with hidden yokai nature
similar_yokai.difference: Rokurokubi have extending necks, different curse origin

sources.title: The Book of Yokai: Mysterious Creatures of Japanese Folklore
The Night Parade of One Hundred Demons: A Field Guide to Japanese Yokai
